annotations.label: Commutative diagram

anchor.snippet: a commutative diagram is a diagram such that all directed paths in the diagram with the same start and endpoints lead to the same result

annotations.note: Mathlib has no generic 'commutative diagram' predicate; only the special case of commutative squares is bundled (`CommSq`, with bundled variant `Square`).

annotations.label: Parts of a commutative diagram

anchor.snippet: A commutative diagram often consists of three parts

annotations.note: This is meta-mathematical description of diagram presentation and is not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Monomorphism arrow

anchor.snippet: A monomorphism may be labeled with a

annotations.note: Arrow decorations are notational; the underlying concept `Mono` is formalized in Mathlib but the visual hooked-arrow convention itself is not.

annotations.label: Epimorphism arrow

anchor.snippet: An epimorphism may be labeled with a

annotations.note: The underlying notion `Epi` is formalized; the two-headed arrow notation is presentational only.

annotations.label: Isomorphism arrow

anchor.snippet: An isomorphism may be labeled with a

annotations.note: `IsIso`/`Iso` formalize isomorphisms; the `≅` decoration on arrows is presentational.

annotations.label: Dashed arrow (existence)

anchor.snippet: The dashed arrow typically represents the claim that the indicated morphism exists

annotations.note: Dashed-arrow conventions are visual notation without a direct Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Natural transformation arrow

anchor.snippet: If the morphism acts between two arrows

annotations.note: Natural transformations are formalized as `NatTrans`; the double-arrow drawing convention is presentational.

annotations.label: Commutativity of polygonal subdiagrams

anchor.snippet: a diagram is commutative if every polygonal subdiagram is commutative

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general theorem reducing commutativity of a diagram to commutativity of subpolygons.

annotations.label: First isomorphism theorem diagram

anchor.snippet: In the left diagram, which expresses the first isomorphism theorem

annotations.note: The first isomorphism theorem for groups is `QuotientGroup.quotientKerEquivRange`, with analogous versions in module/ring files.

annotations.label: Three equalities diagram

anchor.snippet: In order for the diagram below to commute, three equalities must be satisfied

annotations.note: This is an illustrative ad-hoc diagram and has no dedicated Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Diagram chasing

anchor.snippet: Diagram chasing (also called diagrammatic search ) is a method of mathematical proof used especially in homological algebra

annotations.note: Diagram chasing is a proof methodology, not an object; Mathlib proves diagram lemmas but does not name the technique.

annotations.label: Diagram chasing lemmas

anchor.snippet: Examples of proofs by diagram chasing include those typically given for the five lemma

annotations.note: The five lemma is formalized both for modules (`Mathlib.Algebra.FiveLemma`) and abelian categories (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.DiagramLemmas.Four`), with the snake lemma in `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ShortComplex.SnakeLemma`.

annotations.label: Cat as a 2-category

anchor.snippet: the category of small categories Cat is naturally a 2-category

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `Cat.bicategory` together with `Cat.bicategory.strict`, exhibiting `Cat` as a strict bicategory (i.e. a 2-category).

annotations.label: Vertical and horizontal composition in a 2-category

anchor.snippet: There are two kinds of composition in a 2-category

annotations.note: Vertical composition is the `homCategory` composition and horizontal composition is given by `whiskerLeft`/`whiskerRight` plus 1-cell composition in the `Bicategory` class.

annotations.label: Diagram as a functor

anchor.snippet: A commutative diagram in a category C can be interpreted as a functor from an index category J to C

annotations.note: Mathlib uniformly identifies diagrams of shape `J` in `C` with functors `J ⥤ C` (e.g. as cones over such functors in the limits library).

annotations.label: Commutative diagram from poset category

anchor.snippet: a commutative diagram is a visualization of a diagram indexed by a poset category

annotations.note: Preorders/posets carry the canonical thin category instance `Preorder.smallCategory`, so functors out of them are automatically commutative diagrams, but this informal correspondence is not named.

annotations.label: Poset category from commutative diagram

anchor.snippet: given a commutative diagram, it defines a poset category

annotations.note: There is no Mathlib construction taking an arbitrary commutative diagram and returning its underlying poset/thin category.

annotations.label: Non-commuting diagrams (free quiver)

anchor.snippet: the diagram of a single object with an endomorphism

annotations.note: Mathlib has the free category `Paths` on a quiver (`PathCategory/Basic`), of which the single-loop quiver case is an instance, but this specific example is not isolated.
